content.problemStatement:
Little Elephant from Zoo of Lviv likes to watch movies.

There are **N** different movies (numbered from **0** to **N − 1**) he wants to watch in some order. Of course, he will watch each movie exactly once. The priority of **i**th movie is **Pi**.

A watching of a movie is called exciting if and only if one of the following two conditions holds:


This is the first watching.
The priority of this movie is strictly greater than the maximal priority of the movies watched so far.
Let us call the number of exciting watchings the excitingness of the order.

Help him to find the number of different watching orders whose excitingness does not exceed **K**. Since the answer can be large, print it modulo **1000000007 (109+7)**.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two lines. The first line contains two space-separated integers N and K. The second line contains N space-separated integers P1, P2, ..., PN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of different watching orders having at most K excitingness modulo 1000000007 (10^9+7).

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, there are two permutations with at most one excitingness: [3, 1, 2] and [3, 2, 1]. For the second test case, all 24 permutations have at most three excitingness.
There are 16 permutations with at most two excitingness.
There is only one permutation with at most one excitingness: [3, 3, 3].

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using dynamic programming. We maintain a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] represents the number of ways to watch the first i movies with at most j excitingness. We iterate over each movie and for each possible excitingness, we calculate the number of ways to watch the movie. We use modular arithmetic to avoid overflow.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * K) per test case, O(T * N * K) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * K) — the dominant structure is the dp array.
